methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Anyon Systems is a Series A quantum computing company that has raised C$23M+ to date. The quantum computing sector comprises a sparse comparable universe: three public companies (IONQ, RGTI, QBTS) and eight private peers including IonQ, Quantinuum, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum, and Infleqtion. This limited set constrains traditional valuation approaches.

Standard peer-median analysis is not applicable here. The quantum computing space lacks sufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables with similar valuation tiers to support a robust median multiple. Of the seven same-sector peers identified, filtering for stage, maturity, and scale yields fewer than the three minimum datapoints required for statistical reliability. An all-sector median approach would introduce excessive noise given the heterogeneous funding stages and business models across the broader comp set.

The absence of a triangulated range reflects genuine data scarcity rather than analytical failure. Any valuation of Anyon Systems at this stage would require either: (i) acceptance of wide bands derived from the broader public and private peer set despite stage heterogeneity, or (ii) a shift to early-stage quantum venture benchmarks outside the direct peer universe. Key uncertainties include the company's IP differentiation relative to Quantinuum and IonQ, near-term technical validation milestones, and capital efficiency relative to the sector's historical burn profiles.
